title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — June 27, 2026
headline: Lebanon-Israel ceasefire framework signed June 26, rejected by Hezbollah within hours — neither score changes.
summary: June 27 is a confirmation-dominant cycle: 15 assessments, zero score changes, and 11 false-alarm signals stopped — a new single-night record, eclipsing the eight from the night before. The lead story is a diplomatic agreement that did not hold. Secretary Rubio announced an Israel-Lebanon framework on June 26. Lebanon's government and Israel signed. Hezbollah rejected it within hours on June 27 as 'humiliation, disgrace, and surrender of sovereignty.' The war continues. No Israeli withdrawal is required under the agreement. An unimplemented, contested document is not realized positive own-conduct. Lebanon holds at 17.2 of 100. Israel holds at 0 of 100. Two US policy developments introduce watch flags without moving the score. The Justice Department issued a memo questioning 27 years of disability-integration law — the Olmstead mandate that people with disabilities live in communities, not institutions. A federal appeals court separately allowed fast-track deportations to resume nationwide. The United States holds at 17.5 of 100. Watch flags are now open on the Equity and Accountability dimensions. The Democratic Republic of the Congo's (DRC) Ebola outbreak added 37 more cases in two days — now 1,155 confirmed cases and 304 deaths. The July natural peak is now within days. In Somalia, a second district (Baidoa) has now joined Buur Hakaba at extreme acute malnutrition. No formal famine declaration has been issued — that remains the scored change trigger. Sudan's El Obeid siege continued into day 15-plus of RSF drone strikes. Iran executed prisoners throughout its nuclear diplomacy window. Two entities outside tonight's top-15 also produced signals. Niger's UN Working Group found journalist Moussa Tiangari's detention unlawful on June 23. A whistleblower lawsuit against Meta — filed by former executive Sarah Wynn-Williams on June 25 — is pre-adjudication and logged for a future assessment.

topSignals.title: Lebanon 17.2 of 100: Framework Signed June 26, Rejected by Hezbollah Within Hours — War Continues
topSignals.whyItMatters: A US-brokered ceasefire framework was signed June 26 and rejected by Hezbollah within hours as 'surrender of sovereignty.' The war continues. An unimplemented agreement rejected by a key armed actor is not realized progress — Lebanon's score holds at 17.2 of 100.
topSignals.description: Lebanon holds at 17.2 of 100. Secretary of State Rubio announced a US-brokered framework on June 26. Lebanon's government and Israel signed; the US co-signed. Hezbollah's Secretary-General Naim Qassem announced on June 27 that the deal 'crosses all red lines' and constitutes 'humiliation, disgrace, and surrender of sovereignty.' Hezbollah pledged to keep fighting. The war continues: more than 4,000 people have been killed and 1.2 million displaced in Lebanon since March 2. The framework ties any ceasefire to Hezbollah disarmament and does not mandate Israeli withdrawal. An unimplemented agreement that lacks the acceptance of a key governing-coalition actor is a forward trigger — a condition that, if met, would produce scored positive own-conduct — not a realized outcome. The benchmark's attribution rule also applies: the ongoing civilian harm in Lebanon results from Israel's conduct. Lebanon is scored on what Lebanon does, not what is done to it. The forward trigger: Lebanese Armed Forces (LAF) assuming sovereign control of southern Lebanon, a durable ceasefire, and reconstruction delivery would be scorable positive own-conduct in a future window.

evidence.claim: Hezbollah Secretary-General Qassem rejected the Rubio framework June 27 as 'humiliation, disgrace, and surrender of sovereignty' and pledged to keep fighting; ceasefire remains absent.
CNBC June 26 reports Rubio announced framework signed by Israel, Lebanon, and the US; deal ties ceasefire to Hezbollah disarmament and does not mandate Israeli withdrawal.
UN News June 2026: war continues — 4,000-plus killed, 1.2 million displaced in Lebanon since March 2, 2026; 12 children killed or maimed daily.

topSignals.title: United States 17.5 of 100: DOJ Memo Questions 27-Year Disability-Integration Law — Watch Flags Raised
topSignals.whyItMatters: The Justice Department questioned the Olmstead ruling — the law requiring disabled Americans to live in communities, not institutions — in a June 20 memo. No one has been re-institutionalized yet. But the signal is the largest disability-rights policy reversal documented in this benchmark series. The score holds while the watch is open.
topSignals.description: The United States holds at 17.5 of 100. The Department of Justice (DOJ) Office of Legal Counsel issued a memo on June 20 questioning the Olmstead integration mandate — the Supreme Court's 1999 ruling requiring states to provide services to people with disabilities in the most integrated setting appropriate. Disability advocates described the memo as potentially allowing states to re-institutionalize people who currently live in their communities with federal support. AAPD called the memo 'horrifying.' A federal appeals court separately allowed the administration to resume fast-track deportations nationwide on June 24, citing a government appeal. About 66,000 people are now held in ICE detention — an all-time high. The US score of 17.5 was set on June 9 through the US government's own conduct in a Developing-to-Critical band crossing. That baseline is recent and own-conduct-driven. The Olmstead memo is policy intent (a legal-opinion document); the deportation resumption continues conduct already priced in the June 9 score; the detention figure is an incremental rise. The aggregate is sub-threshold. Two watch flags are now formally open: (1) Equity (EQU) — formal DOJ withdrawal of Olmstead enforcement or documented state re-institutionalization with federal backing; (2) Accountability (ACC) — realized harms from resumed fast-track removals of people with legitimate legal claims.

evidence.claim: NPR June 20: DOJ OLC memo questions the Olmstead integration mandate; AAPD describes the memo as 'horrifying'; advocates warn states could institutionalize people currently living in communities with federal support.
STAT News June 22: DOJ memo targets disability integration Olmstead mandate — analysis of legal implications of the Office of Legal Counsel opinion on community placement requirements.
NPR June 24: federal appeals court allows administration to resume fast-track deportations nationwide; critics cite error-proneness and due-process erosion.

topSignals.title: Sudan 0 of 100: El Obeid Siege Passes Day 15 — RSF Drones Strike Shelters; UNSC Warning Unactioned
topSignals.whyItMatters: RSF drone strikes hit displacement shelters and a funeral in a besieged city of 500,000 people. The UN Security Council warned of imminent mass atrocities eight days ago. More than 50 civilians have been killed. Sudan is at the lowest possible score. Evidence is not retreating.
topSignals.description: Sudan holds at 0 of 100. The Rapid Support Forces' (RSF) drone campaign against El Obeid in North Kordofan entered its 15th day. The drones have struck power substations, fuel stations, displacement shelters, and a funeral gathering. Human Rights Watch published an action brief on June 26 demanding UN Security Council action. The UNSC issued a non-binding statement on June 20 warning of 'imminent risk of mass atrocities' — and no enforcement resolution followed. More than 50 civilians have been killed in drone strikes. About 500,000 civilians remain in El Obeid; about 100,000 have been internally displaced within the city. Famine has been declared in El-Fasher and Kadugli. The RSF's documented pattern — drone strikes on infrastructure, then direct assault — killed roughly 6,000 people in El-Fasher in three days in May 2025. Sudan is at the absolute floor; no lower score is possible. The current evidence reinforces but cannot lower the floor.

evidence.quote: Sudan: Urgently Address the Situation In and Around El Obeid, Take Bold Steps Towards Peace
evidence.claim: HRW June 26 action brief: RSF drone strikes day 15-plus on power substations, fuel stations, displacement shelters, and a funeral gathering; more than 50 civilians killed; UNSC action demanded.
UNSC June 20 non-binding statement warned of imminent risk of mass atrocities; 500,000-plus civilians besieged; 100,000-plus internally displaced within El Obeid; no enforcement resolution issued.

topSignals.title: DRC 2.3 of 100: Ebola Climbs to 1,155 Cases and 304 Deaths — July Peak Now Days Away
topSignals.whyItMatters: The DRC Ebola outbreak added 37 cases in two days. The July natural peak is now within days. One province accounts for 91 percent of all cases. There is no approved vaccine. The DRC government's contact tracing stands at 45 percent — half the rate needed to stop transmission.
topSignals.description: The Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) holds at 2.3 of 100. As of June 25, the Bundibugyo-strain Ebola outbreak has 1,155 confirmed cases and 304 deaths, up from 1,118 cases and 291 deaths on June 24. Ituri province accounts for 1,054 of the 1,155 cases. The World Health Organization (WHO) declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC) on May 16. A humanitarian doctor in France tested positive on June 24 — the first confirmed EU-imported case; Germany reported a second imported case the same week. No approved vaccine exists for the Bundibugyo strain. Contact tracing stands at 45 percent, far below the 90 percent needed to stop transmission. More than 70 health workers have been infected, degrading response capacity. The DRC government's failure to achieve adequate contact tracing is own-conduct evidence scored on the Awareness (AWR) and Action (ACT) dimensions. The near-floor score of 2.3 reflects collapsed state capacity. The July natural Ebola peak window is now within days. The mass killings in eastern DRC — more than 1,500 civilians since December — are attributed to Rwanda-backed M23 forces and are scored against Rwanda, not the DRC.

evidence.claim: WHO June 25, 2026: 1,155 confirmed cases and 304 deaths in the DRC Ebola outbreak (Bundibugyo strain); 1,054 of 1,155 cases in Ituri province; contact tracing at 45 percent; WHO PHEIC since May 16; no approved vaccine.
ECDC June 2026: outbreak data and European case confirmation — first EU-imported case in France (humanitarian doctor); second imported case in Germany.

topSignals.title: Somalia 4.7 of 100: Baidoa Now Also at Extreme Malnutrition — Famine Declaration Window Still Open
topSignals.whyItMatters: A second Somali district — Baidoa — has now reached the same extreme acute malnutrition level as Buur Hakaba. No formal famine has been declared. That declaration is the scored change trigger for Somalia. The WFP reaches only one in ten people who need food.
topSignals.description: Somalia holds at 4.7 of 100. The EU/JRC Famine Review confirms that Baidoa has joined Buur Hakaba at IPC AMN Phase 5 — extreme acute malnutrition. Together, the two districts in Bay region face the highest concentration of acute malnutrition documented in Somalia this cycle. The IPC alert frames the situation as 'famine projected in two districts' under a worst-case scenario. As of June 27, the IPC/FSNAU has not issued a formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration — that formal declaration is the benchmark's scored change trigger. The trigger works this way: a precursor metric (malnutrition rate above the famine threshold) is credible evidence of deterioration, but it is not the same as a concluded IPC determination that famine exists in a specific area. About 6.7 million Somalis face high acute food insecurity. About 1.9 million children are acutely malnourished. WFP is reaching only one in ten people who need food. Al-Shabaab continues to obstruct food deliveries. The famine risk window extends credibly through September 2026 in Bay, Bakool, and Gedo regions.

evidence.claim: IPC alert June 2026: Buur Hakaba and Baidoa both at IPC AMN Phase 5; IPC projects famine under worst-case scenario; no formal Phase 5 famine declaration issued as of June 27.
EU/JRC Famine Review June 2026 confirms Buur Hakaba and Baidoa at IPC AMN Phase 5 — famine risk projection, not an official declaration.
Action Against Hunger June 2026: GAM 37.1 percent in Buur Hakaba; most malnutrition facilities closed; WFP reaching one in ten people who need food; 6.7 million facing high acute food insecurity.

topSignals.title: Israel 0 of 100: UN Genocide Finding and Continuing Civilian Deaths Reinforce the Floor — Lebanon Framework Does Not Change the Score
topSignals.whyItMatters: The UN Commission of Inquiry found Israel committed genocide by deliberately targeting Palestinian children — about 20,000 killed. The Lebanon ceasefire framework does not require Israeli withdrawal. The war continues in both Gaza and Lebanon. Israel is at the lowest possible score. No lower score exists.
topSignals.description: Israel holds at 0 of 100. The UN Commission of Inquiry (report A/HRC/62/CRP.2, June 23) found that Israel 'continues to commit genocide and other atrocity crimes by deliberately targeting Palestinian children.' About 20,000 Palestinian children have been killed and 44,000 injured since October 2023. In Gaza, the ceasefire remains fragile — 936 fatalities since the October 2025 ceasefire. The Kerem Shalom crossing is congested and the Zikim crossing has been closed for two weeks. In Lebanon, the Rubio framework signed June 26 does not mandate Israeli withdrawal. Hezbollah rejected the framework June 27. The war continues; 4,000-plus people have been killed and 1.2 million displaced in Lebanon since March 2. The floor score of 0 of 100 reflects the most severe designation. No lower value is possible. Both new developments — the genocide determination and the contested framework — reinforce the floor but cannot lower it. The genocide finding is the strongest single-cycle evidence-tier reinforcement in the benchmark's record.

evidence.claim: Al Jazeera June 23 reporting on UN COI report A/HRC/62/CRP.2: Israel's deliberate targeting of children constitutes genocide and atrocity crimes; approximately 20,000 Palestinian children killed, 44,000 injured since October 2023.
UN News June 2026: Gaza ceasefire fragile — 936 fatalities since October 2025 ceasefire; one Palestinian child killed daily on average; Kerem Shalom congested; Zikim crossing closed two weeks.

topSignals.title: Anthropic 59.1 of 100: Day 16 of Export-Control Suspension — Commerce Silence Continues, EU AI Act in 36 Days
topSignals.whyItMatters: The US government's suspension of two Anthropic AI models is now in its 16th day. The June 26 Congressional deadline for a Commerce explanation passed with no public response. The export-control order is the government's action — not Anthropic's own conduct. Anthropic's score holds at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band.
topSignals.description: Anthropic holds at 59.1 of 100. Day 16 of the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) export-control order forcing the global suspension of Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 — the first export controls ever applied to a commercially deployed AI model. No public response from Commerce Secretary Lutnick followed the June 26 Congressional deadline set by four bipartisan House members. The Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models remain suspended globally, including for Anthropic employees who are foreign nationals. The benchmark's attribution rule: the US government took this action. It is scored against the US government's record, not Anthropic's. Anthropic also hired Nobel laureate John Jumper from DeepMind (June 20); talent recruitment is not scored as compassion conduct. Anthropic sits 0.9 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary (60.0). EU AI Act full applicability on August 2 — 36 days away — is the next structured compliance milestone for AI labs.

evidence.claim: AI Weekly June 27 confirms Day 16 of the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 suspension; June 26 Congressional deadline elapsed with no public Commerce Department response; suspension remains in effect globally.
Volkov Law blog June 25-27: analysis of the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 export-control order — when the government pulls the plug on AI governance, what precedent is set?

boundaryWatch.trigger: Next US Commerce or BIS action on the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 export-control order, or a congressional hearing; EU AI Act full applicability on August 2.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits 0.9 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary at 60.0. Seventh consecutive cycle in this range. Day 16 of the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 suspension. The June 26 Congressional deadline passed without a public Commerce response. EU AI Act applicability on August 2 is the next external milestone.

boundaryWatch.trigger: NLRB review of the unfair-labor-practice charge filed after Apple closed its Towson, Maryland store — its first unionized store. An NLRB complaint or ruling is the conversion trigger.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits 0.6 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary at 60.0. The machinists' union charge alleges union workers received different relocation terms than non-union workers at simultaneously closing stores. The charge has not yet resulted in an NLRB complaint. Third boundary-watch cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: July natural Ebola peak window; M23 territory overlap with WHO response corridors in North Kivu is the floor-crossing trigger if health-response obstruction is documented.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC at 2.3 Critical through cycle 14 of the Ebola emergency. 1,155 cases and 304 deaths as of June 25 — up from 1,118 and 291 two days earlier. Ituri province accounts for 1,054 of 1,155 cases. First EU-imported case in France (June 24). Contact tracing at 45 percent. July peak is days away.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Famine declaration covering Buur Hakaba or Baidoa. Both districts now at IPC AMN Phase 5. A formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration is the scored change trigger.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia at 4.7 Critical. Buur Hakaba and Baidoa both at IPC AMN Phase 5 — extreme acute malnutrition. IPC projects famine under a worst-case scenario. No formal declaration as of June 27. Famine risk window extends through September 2026.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Coordinator-level Sahel-band calibration review. Burkina Faso (6.3) was reassessed in June on comparable UN-documented state-perpetration conduct. Mali's seed score of 12.5 is above the likely calibrated range.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali at 12.5 Critical on a starting-point seed score. Not assessed this cycle. The Sahel calibration flag is in its fourth day. The gap with Burkina Faso (6.3, reweighted June 1 on active-state-perpetration conduct) persists. Coordinator review covers Mali, Niger, and potentially Chad.

boundaryWatch.trigger: DOJ False Claims Act complaint (kickbacks and disability discrimination) and Barrows v. Humana class action (nH Predict AI claim denials). Either concluded finding confirms the Developing-band designation.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana at 35.2 Developing, 4.8 points below the Functional boundary. Sixth cycle post-downgrade. Not assessed this cycle. Neither the DOJ complaint nor the Barrows class action has a fixed hearing date.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Bengali Muslim and Rohingya expulsions documented in June 2026; detect-delete-deport digital surveillance continuing. Not assessed this cycle.
boundaryWatch.note: India at 15.6 Critical sits 0.6 points above the Critical floor through a thirteenth consecutive monitoring cycle. Not assessed tonight.

emergingRisks.risk: The Lebanon-Israel framework is signed but rejected by Hezbollah on the same day — the agreement's status is contested and the war continues. The framework's implementation window is now open. If LAF assumes sovereign control of southern Lebanon, that is the positive-conduct trigger; if the war escalates under conditions Lebanon's government accepted, that adds to Lebanon's own-conduct record.
emergingRisks.detail: Hezbollah's immediate rejection means the framework's implementation path runs through political negotiations that have not yet produced a ceasefire. The war's civilian toll — 4,000-plus killed, 1.2 million displaced in Lebanon since March 2 — continues to be scored against Israel. Lebanon is scored on what Lebanon does; implementation evidence will drive future scoring.

emergingRisks.risk: DRC Ebola is now 1,155 cases and 304 deaths — up 37 cases in two days. The July natural Bundibugyo peak is within days. Ituri province accounts for 91 percent of all cases. First EU-imported case in France (June 24). No approved vaccine. Contact tracing at 45 percent — half the rate needed to stop transmission.
emergingRisks.detail: More than 70 health workers are infected, degrading response capacity. M23-controlled territory overlaps with WHO response corridors in North Kivu — documented obstruction by M23 would extend Rwanda's perpetrator attribution to a secondary pathway. The DRC government's own contact-tracing failure (45 percent) remains the scored own-conduct driver.

emergingRisks.risk: Sudan's RSF drone campaign against El Obeid has passed Day 15. The UN Security Council warned of imminent mass atrocities on June 20 with no enforcement follow-up. The RSF's documented pattern — drone strikes then direct assault — killed roughly 6,000 civilians in El-Fasher in three days in May 2025.
emergingRisks.detail: More than 500,000 civilians remain in El Obeid with hospitals offline. Drone strikes have now hit power substations, fuel stations, displacement shelters, and a funeral gathering — more than 50 civilians killed. RSF posture has not changed following the HRW action brief or the 29-country UNHRC warning.

emergingRisks.risk: Somalia now has two districts — Buur Hakaba and Baidoa — at IPC AMN Phase 5 extreme acute malnutrition. The EU/JRC Famine Review projects famine under a worst-case scenario. A formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration is the scored change trigger for Somalia. The famine risk window extends through September 2026.
emergingRisks.detail: WFP is reaching one in ten people who need food. Al-Shabaab continues to obstruct deliveries. The lean-season peak in August will compound the situation. Each new IPC Phase 5 designation for a Somali location is a scored change trigger.

emergingRisks.risk: The DOJ Olmstead memo signals the largest rollback of disability-integration law in 27 years. States could move to re-institutionalize people currently living in communities with federal support. The EQU and ACC watch flags for the United States are now formally open.
emergingRisks.detail: The Olmstead integration mandate has governed disability services in the US since the Supreme Court's 1999 ruling. The DOJ OLC memo is legal opinion, not yet action. The conversion trigger: formal DOJ enforcement withdrawal or a documented state institutionalization event with federal backing. No such action has occurred as of June 27.

emergingRisks.risk: OpenAI faces simultaneous proceedings from 42 state attorneys general and a Florida criminal investigation. A settlement or adverse finding in any one of these would move OpenAI's score from its current 27.5 of 100.
emergingRisks.detail: The 42-state subpoena covers data practices, handling of minors and seniors, model sycophancy, and internal safety policies. The Florida criminal investigation is tied to the FSU shooting. Both proceedings are ongoing with no fixed hearing dates.

emergingRisks.risk: The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable on August 2 — 36 days away. General-purpose AI transparency obligations, Article 50 labeling, and whistleblower protections activate. xAI and OpenAI have not published compliance roadmaps as of this cycle.
emergingRisks.detail: A post-applicability non-compliance finding for general-purpose AI models constitutes a Tier-5 methodological finding and would move scores. Anthropic's EU AI Act compliance infrastructure is documented. The gap between compliant and non-compliant labs will become legally significant on August 2.

emergingRisks.risk: Mali's enforced-disappearance pattern — documented by UN Special Procedures as potentially constituting crimes against humanity — remains unresolved in the Sahel calibration framework. The Sahel calibration flag is in its fourth consecutive day. Mali holds a seed score of 12.5 compared with Burkina Faso's reweighted 6.3.
emergingRisks.detail: The coordinator-level review covers Mali, Niger, and potentially Chad. New evidence (Bamako opposition disappearances, May 2026) adds to the documented pattern. No timeline is set for the review.

emergingRisks.risk: Niger's UN Working Group on Arbitrary Detention found the detention of journalist Moussa Tiangari unlawful on June 23 — Opinion No. 19/2026. Niger was not in tonight's top-15 priority list.
emergingRisks.detail: The UN Working Group finding adds to a pattern of documented political detention in Niger under the post-coup government. Niger is in the Sahel calibration scope and logged for a future rotation assessment pass.

sectorAlerts.headline: Eight entities at or near the absolute floor; Lebanon framework rejection and Ebola case-count rise are the new developments this cycle.
observations: Sudan (0 of 100): El Obeid siege entered Day 15-plus. HRW action brief (June 26) demanded UNSC action. More than 50 civilians killed in drone strikes on displacement shelters and a funeral. UNSC mass-atrocity warning (June 20) remains unactioned.
Israel (0 of 100): UN Commission of Inquiry genocide finding (June 23) reinforces the floor. Lebanon framework does not mandate Israeli withdrawal. War continues in both Gaza and Lebanon.

Yemen (0 of 100): WFP forced termination of all 365 northern-area staff contracts documented across three consecutive cycles. The humanitarian-access collapse under Houthi governance is the scored own-conduct.

South Sudan (0 of 100): UNICEF confirms 7.8 million face high acute food insecurity (56 percent of population); 700,000 children at risk of dying from severe wasting; four counties at famine risk. State obstruction of aid continues.

DRC (2.3 of 100): 1,155 cases and 304 deaths — up 37 in two days. Ituri province accounts for 91 percent of cases. July peak days away. Contact tracing at 45 percent. First EU-imported case in France (June 24).

Iran (2.5 of 100): 784-plus executions year-to-date — a 37-year high. Executions continued through the Islamabad nuclear framework period (June 14-17). Diplomatic engagement on one dimension does not constitute positive own-conduct on another.

Somalia (4.7 of 100): Baidoa has now joined Buur Hakaba at IPC AMN Phase 5. Two adjacent districts face extreme acute malnutrition. No formal famine declaration — that remains the scored change trigger.
Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100): UN CERD extrajudicial-killing findings and Sahel lean-season food crisis reinforce the near-floor. Sahel calibration flag now in its fourth consecutive day.

sectorAlerts.headline: Agreement signed and rejected within 24 hours — a methodology case study in what 'implementation' means for scoring.
observations: Lebanon (17.2 of 100) was assessed this cycle on the Rubio framework. The framework passed the benchmark's two §3e-bis screening checks: (1) directionality — the framework is unimplemented and does not yet constitute positive own-conduct; (2) attribution — the ongoing war's civilian harm is Israel's conduct, not Lebanon's.
Hezbollah's same-day rejection means the framework has not produced a ceasefire, a withdrawal, or LAF territorial control. The forward trigger — implementation — remains open.

The Lebanon case establishes what the benchmark means by 'forward trigger': a signed agreement creates a condition that, if met through implementation, would be scored positive own-conduct. The agreement itself is not the conduct.
Israel (0 of 100): the Lebanon framework does not mandate Israeli withdrawal and does not constitute positive own-conduct for Israel. The war continues; the floor holds.

sectorAlerts.headline: Anthropic Day 16 of export-control suspension; EU AI Act applicability in 36 days; the gap between labs remains at 59.1 points.
observations: Anthropic (59.1 of 100, Functional band): Day 16 of the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 suspension. Commerce Department's silence after the June 26 Congressional deadline continues. The export-control order is the US government's action — scored against the US, not Anthropic. Nobel laureate John Jumper joining from DeepMind is a talent signal, not scored conduct.
OpenAI (27.5 of 100, Developing band): 42-state AG subpoena and Florida criminal investigation carry forward as pre-adjudication. No new concluded proceedings this cycle.

EU AI Act full applicability is 36 days away. xAI and OpenAI have not published compliance roadmaps. Anthropic's compliance infrastructure is documented. The compliance gap will become legally significant on August 2.
The AI-lab score gap — Anthropic 59.1 of 100 at the top versus xAI 0 of 100 at the floor — is 59.1 points. This gap has held for at least seven consecutive cycles.

sectorAlerts.sector: Screening Discipline — Eleven False Positives (New Record)
sectorAlerts.headline: Eleven signals stopped in one night — the third consecutive day that the record was set and broken.
observations: Lebanon (two screens): the ceasefire framework surfaced as a candidate positive-conduct signal; §3e-bis(3) directionality and attribution checks fired simultaneously — the first time both screens fired on the same signal for the same entity.

United States (three-rule compound): DOJ Olmstead memo is pre-implementation (forward trigger); deportation resumption partly double-counts already-scored conduct; stale-baseline claim is invalid because the June 9 baseline is own-conduct-driven. This is the second three-rule compound screen in the June 2026 series after Anthropic on June 26.
Anthropic (three-rule compound again): attribution, forward-trigger, and stale-cache all confirmed for a second consecutive cycle. The compound screen applied consistently across both cycles.

Pre-adjudication holds (OpenAI, UnitedHealth, Apple): investigations, probes, and charges are not concluded proceedings. The pre-adjudication rule held for the fourth or fifth consecutive cycle for each of these entities.
Israel floor-limitation and DRC attribution: both screened as they have in prior cycles — floor-limitation stops the score moving below zero; M23 harm is attributed to Rwanda, not DRC.

Nigeria double-count and Somalia threshold-check: both screened identically to prior cycles. The double-count rule (Borno Phase 5 already priced June 19) and the threshold-check (no formal famine declaration) each fired for the third or more consecutive cycle.

sectorAlerts.sector: United States — Disability Rights and Deportation Watch Flags
sectorAlerts.headline: Two new watch flags opened on Equity and Accountability — the DOJ Olmstead memo is the most significant disability-policy reversal signal in this benchmark series.
observations: The United States (17.5 of 100, Critical band) confirmed this cycle with two new watch flags — the first time watch flags have been formally opened for the US since the June 9 band crossing.

The DOJ Olmstead memo: the Supreme Court's 1999 Olmstead v. L.C. ruling requires states to serve people with disabilities in the most integrated setting appropriate. The DOJ OLC memo questions this mandate. No formal enforcement action has occurred — the memo is policy intent. The Equity (EQU) watch flag tracks whether states begin re-institutionalizing people.

Fast-track deportation resumption: a federal appeals court allowed the administration to resume nationwide fast-track deportations. The June 9 score already priced the pattern; this is the litigation continuing on already-priced conduct. The Accountability (ACC) watch flag tracks whether realized harms flow from resumed removals.
US ICE detention at 66,000 — an all-time high. This is incremental (up from roughly 60,000 in May). Not a threshold crossing; logged as a trending indicator.

methodologyNotes.name: Contested Agreements and the Forward-Trigger Doctrine — Lebanon Framework
methodologyNotes.description: The Lebanon-Israel framework (June 26-27) is the first test in this cycle series of the forward-trigger doctrine applied to a signed diplomatic agreement. The benchmark's rule: a signed agreement that has not been implemented and is not accepted by all parties is a forward trigger, not a realized outcome. The framework was signed by Lebanon's government, Israel, and the US — and rejected within hours by Hezbollah, a governing-coalition actor with control over armed forces operating on Lebanese territory. Neither signing nor rejection constitutes scored own-conduct. Scored positive own-conduct would require Lebanese Armed Forces (LAF) assuming sovereign control of southern Lebanon, a durable ceasefire, and reconstruction delivery. Scored negative own-conduct would require Lebanon's government to take new actions that harm its own population. Neither occurred this cycle.

methodologyNotes.name: Policy Intent vs. Realized Harm — The DOJ Olmstead Memo
methodologyNotes.description: The Justice Department's June 20 memo questioning the Olmstead integration mandate is the clearest example in the June 2026 series of the distinction between policy intent and scored harm. The Olmstead ruling (1999) requires states to provide services to people with disabilities in the most integrated setting appropriate. The DOJ memo is a legal-opinion document signaling the administration's policy direction. No enforcement action has been taken, no state has re-institutionalized any person, and no federal funding has been withdrawn. The benchmark scores realized harms — actions that have already occurred — not policy statements. The watch flags on Equity (EQU) and Accountability (ACC) signal that the conversion triggers are specific and observable: formal DOJ enforcement withdrawal, or a documented state institutionalization event with federal backing.

methodologyNotes.name: Eleven False Positives in One Night — Three-Rule Compound Screens
methodologyNotes.description: Tonight's eleven false positives include a new pattern: two separate entities (Lebanon and the United States) each triggered three §3e-bis rules simultaneously. Lebanon's compound: directionality (the framework is forward-looking) + attribution (war harm is Israel's conduct) + pre-adjudication/forward-trigger (the agreement is unimplemented). The United States' compound: stale-baseline-rejected (the June 9 score is own-conduct-driven and recent) + double-count (deportation conduct already priced) + forward-trigger (Olmstead memo is not realized harm). A three-rule compound screen means the evidence being considered is genuinely ambiguous across multiple failure modes — it looks like a signal, involves the right entity, and comes from credible sources, but fails at each screening rule independently. Three consecutive nights of record false-positive counts (4 on June 25, 8 on June 26, 11 on June 27) reflect growing scanner signal volume against a methodology that is holding correctly.

methodologyNotes.name: Diplomatic Engagement Across Multiple Tracks — Iran's Executions Through the Islamabad Window
methodologyNotes.description: Iran executed prisoners throughout the June 14-17 Islamabad nuclear framework negotiations. The NCRI data shows the execution rate did not slow during or after the diplomatic window. This reflects a standing methodology principle: diplomatic engagement on one scored dimension (external relations) does not constitute positive own-conduct on a separate scored dimension (domestic repression). The benchmark assesses each dimension independently. Iran's nuclear engagement is relevant to the Boundaries (BND) dimension on arms non-proliferation conduct, which is already scored. The execution surge is scored on the Integrity (INT) and Accountability (ACC) dimensions. Movement on one does not offset or credit the other.

forwardTriggers.trigger: Lebanese Armed Forces (LAF) assuming sovereign control of southern Lebanese territory as a result of the Rubio framework or successor agreement. A durable ceasefire, LAF deployment, and civilian access restoration together would constitute positive own-conduct for Lebanon (17.2 of 100).
Full resolution-phase reassessment due. Conversion trigger: reversal of the June 8 codified-impunity law and release of detained union leaders.

July natural Bundibugyo Ebola peak window. 1,155 cases and 304 deaths as of June 25. M23 territory overlap with WHO response corridors in North Kivu is the secondary trigger for attributing obstruction to Rwanda.
EU AI Act full applicability — general-purpose AI transparency obligations, Article 50 labeling, and whistleblower protections activate. No public compliance roadmap from xAI as of this cycle.
EU AI Act full applicability — same obligations as above apply. No public compliance roadmap from OpenAI as of this cycle.
EU AI Act full applicability — Anthropic's compliance infrastructure is documented; the applicability date tests whether it holds under enforcement conditions.
August IPC lean-season update — IPC Phase 5 Borno trajectory and government response adequacy. Actual WFP food-assistance cuts and a realized Phase 5 famine declaration would be scored triggers.

Extended famine declaration window — credible risk in Bay, Bakool, and Gedo regions through September 2026. Buur Hakaba and Baidoa both at IPC AMN Phase 5. Each new IPC Phase 5 designation in Somalia is a scored change trigger.

RSF commencement of direct assault on El Obeid residential zones — the El-Fasher pattern of drone strikes then direct assault (6,000 civilians in three days, May 2025) is the documented comparator. Day 15-plus of drone campaign.

Formal DOJ withdrawal of Olmstead enforcement — the EQU conversion trigger. Or: documented state re-institutionalization of a person with disabilities with federal backing — the ACC conversion trigger. Neither has occurred as of June 27.
Coordinator-level Sahel-band calibration review — Mali (12.5) versus reweighted peer Burkina Faso (6.3) on comparable UN-documented state-perpetration conduct. Flag in fourth day; no timeline set.
42-state attorney general proceedings and Florida criminal investigation — no fixed hearing dates; any concluded finding, settlement, or criminal charge constitutes a scored change trigger.

Whether OCHA or IOM can establish alternative food-delivery mechanisms in northern Yemen following WFP's forced termination of all 365 staff contracts. Without food-agency presence, delivery to 22.3 million people is severely constrained.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: Lebanon's government signed the Rubio framework June 26; Hezbollah rejected it within hours as 'surrender.' If Lebanese Armed Forces establish sovereign control in southern Lebanon, does that constitute positive own-conduct under the Boundaries dimension, independent of Hezbollah's disarmament status?
dailyOpeningQuestion.tensionFraming: Agreement signed and rejected within 24 hours — the framework tests whether signing constitutes conduct when the signatories lack sovereignty over key territory.
